title: fromNodeReadable has no idempotent cancel guard, allowing double session.cancel on concurrent abort paths
description: fromReadableStream and fromByteRange both use a `cancelled` flag + `cancelBoth` closure to guarantee session.cancel is called at most once. fromNodeReadable lacks this guard entirely. If the AbortSignal fires concurrently while the loop is at the `signal?.aborted` break (line 43) and the code reaches `await session.cancel(ABORT_REASON)` at line 63, the `onAbort` handler (line 22) may also be mid-execution calling `session.cancel`. Two concurrent cancel calls can hit already-torn-down session state depending on the implementation. The pattern is already established in browser.ts and should be applied here for consistency and safety.

title: toReadableStream cancel() swallows iterator.return() rejection via finally supersession
description: In the ReadableStream underlying source `cancel()` handler, `await iterator.return()` runs inside a try block whose `finally` calls `await cancelSession(r)`. If `iterator.return()` throws, the exception is propagated to the `finally` block â€” but if `cancelSession` itself throws (or resolves cleanly), JS `finally` semantics cause the original throw to be silently dropped and replaced by the finally outcome. The caller of `stream.cancel()` may therefore never learn that iterator cleanup failed, masking bugs where the encode session was not cleanly terminated.

title: parseNonNegativeInt silently loses precision for byte offsets > Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER
description: Content-Range and Content-Length headers for very large files (> 9 PB) are parsed with `Number(s)` which loses precision for integers beyond 2^53-1. The parsed `fullSize`, `start`, or `end` values would be silently wrong, making the range validation (line 550-554) and underDelivered logic (line 681) produce incorrect results. While 9 PB files are rare today, the function gives no indication that values are clamped. BigInt parsing or a size cap check with an explicit error would prevent silent corruption.

title: fromByteRange uses globalThis.fetch as default but does not guard against it being undefined
description: The default for `fetchImpl` is `globalThis.fetch` (line 442 destructuring). In Node.js < 18 or environments where the Fetch API is not available, `globalThis.fetch` is `undefined`. The function would then throw `TypeError: fetchImpl is not a function` inside the try/catch block at line 476, which would call `session.cancel(e.message)` and rethrow â€” so session cleanup happens. However, the error message 'fetchImpl is not a function' is cryptic; a pre-flight guard at the top of the function would give a clear actionable error before any async work begins.

title: onAbort in fromNodeReadable swallows session.cancel rejection silently
description: The abort handler fires `void session.cancel(ABORT_REASON).finally(...)`. The `void` discards any rejection from `session.cancel`. While `.finally(() => readable.destroy())` ensures the readable is destroyed even if cancel rejects, the cancel rejection is silently dropped with no logging or propagation. Unlike `fromReadableStream` and `fromByteRange` which use `Promise.allSettled` inside `cancelBoth` (making rejections visible even if not rethrown), this path has no visibility into cancel failures.

title: onHeaders error path in fromByteRange duplicates teardown instead of using the main finally
description: When `onHeaders` throws (lines 572-578), the code manually calls `cancelBoth`, removes the abort listener, and releases the reader lock â€” duplicating what the main try/finally already handles. If `cancelBoth` itself rejects in this manual teardown, `signal.removeEventListener` and `reader.releaseLock()` are still reached because they're sequential statements, but this creates two separate teardown paths that must be kept in sync. A cleaner pattern would be to let the `onHeaders` throw propagate into the main try/catch/finally, eliminating the duplication. As written, if a new cleanup step is added to the main finally, the onHeaders path may be forgotten.

title: No error taxonomy / sentinel error types for stream failure modes
description: All error paths throw plain `Error`, `RangeError`, or `DOMException`. Callers cannot reliably distinguish between a network error, a range validation failure, an ETag mismatch (resume corruption), a budget-exceeded abort, or a malformed Content-Range without string-matching error messages. Adding a small set of typed error classes (e.g. `RangeNotSatisfiedError`, `ResourceChangedError`, `RangeMismatchError`) with a `code` field would let callers handle retry vs. restart vs. abort without fragile message parsing, and would align with the existing protocol event taxonomy in jxl-scheduler.

title: fromByteRange / fromRangePrefix have no built-in timeout on the fetch call or body transfer
description: Both functions accept an AbortSignal but do not provide a default timeout. In mobile/field/offline contexts described in the README, a stalled TCP connection can leave the fetch awaiting indefinitely with no timeout. Callers must remember to wrap their signal with `AbortSignal.timeout()`. A `timeoutMs` option in `RangePrefixOptions` that internally creates a composite `AbortSignal.any([signal, AbortSignal.timeout(timeoutMs)])` would make the timeout opt-in with a sensible field-friendly default pattern, consistent with the `maxBytes` convergedByteEnd cutoff already offered.
